aliceblue: Named after a steamship hue popular in the 1880s rather than after Lewis Carroll's heroine, Alice Blue is barely a colour at all. The hex sits one step from pure white with a faint cyan cast, the kind of background a hospital intranet uses when designers decide #ffffff feels too clinical. It works best as a page wash behind charcoal `#1f2937` body text, where contrast clears AAA without any visual fatigue. Nesting two Alice Blue surfaces side by side is a waste; the eye averages them into a single field within seconds. Treat it as off-white with a personality, not as a real accent.
antiquewhite: Antique White carries the warmth of old paper and varnished wood. The CSS spec inherited it from a Pantone-era palette aimed at interior decorators, and its yellow undertone separates it cleanly from cooler whites like Ghost White or pure white. On screens it works as a calm document background, especially for long-form reading where pure white feels harsh under tungsten light. Pair it with deep brown or matte black type. Avoid putting it next to bright yellows; the warmth gets pulled out and the surface starts to read as dingy rather than considered.
aquamarine: Aquamarine names a pale cyan with a green lean, descending from the gem and the Latin words for sea water. The CSS value mixed in 1987 for X11 is brighter than the real mineral but still reads as tropical shallows. It shows up often in beauty and travel branding where a refreshing-but-soft tone is needed. Use it as a flood color for hero sections or as a tint behind dark navy type. Against white it carries low contrast and should not be used for body text; reserve it for surfaces and decorative shapes.
azure: Azure in CSS is almost pure white with a breath of cyan, not the saturated medieval-manuscript blue the name suggests. The discrepancy traces back to X11's color list, which already had a separate slot for the deeper blue. As a background it reads as cool but barely there, useful for tech-product canvases where pure white feels sterile. The historical association is heraldic, so the word still carries authority even when the rendered color is faint. Pair with darker steel blues or charcoal for legible text. Two azure surfaces side by side will appear identical in low light.
beige: Beige is the color of unbleached wool and, for a stretch of the late twentieth century, almost every office computer. Its undertone is yellow-green, which is why it ages better than pinker neutrals when seen in daylight. Used in editorial and interior design as a warm canvas for richer accents like terracotta or muted navy. On screens, beige softens the eye strain that pure white can cause during extended reading, which is why it shows up in long-form reading apps and reader views. The undertone means cool grays placed next to it will look slightly dirty by comparison.
bisque: Cooks know bisque as the rust-pink of lobster shellfish stock, and the CSS value lands in the same family: a warm, slightly orange off-cream. Cosmetics brands like Glossier built entire packaging systems around tones in this band because the warmth photographs as skin rather than as paper. Try it as a card background paired with deep espresso `#3d2817` type and a single accent of muted teal; the trio reads as editorial without leaning twee. Avoid putting Bisque against a cool grey like Slate 500 - the yellow undertone clashes and the surface starts to look like a colour-management mistake rather than a choice.
black: Black is the absence of light, but on a display it is the full off-state of every pixel. As a UI surface it carries the most weight of any color and so is used sparingly for type, borders, and primary actions on light backgrounds. The aesthetic load is heavy: black implies premium, technical, or austere depending on the typography around it. In dark-mode interfaces, true black saves OLED battery but produces uncomfortable contrast spikes against adjacent UI elements. Most dark UIs use a near-black like #0a0a0a or #18181b instead to avoid the visual jolt at edges.
blanchedalmond: Strip an almond of its papery brown skin and the kernel is exactly this colour: pale, faintly pink, faintly yellow. CSS pulled the name from a 1980s X11 swatch list curated by graphics researcher Paul Raveling, who borrowed liberally from sample books. It rarely earns a place in modern UI palettes but shows up constantly in food photography because cameras render it as warm without going beige. Pair it with a dark cocoa `#2c1810` for menu copy and you have a bakery brand in two colours. Place it next to any cool grey and it loses identity completely, reading as cream that got dirty rather than an intentional surface.
blue: Blue is the most-searched color name on the web by a wide margin. The CSS value #0000ff is pure-channel blue, the bluest blue an sRGB display can show, and it is too saturated and dark for most modern interfaces. Designers usually reach for a softer mid-blue around #2563eb or #3b82f6 instead. Cultural associations are unusually consistent across regions: trust, depth, water, sky, sleep. Use the CSS value sparingly at full saturation. Against dark gray text it can vibrate; against white the contrast is high enough to feel sharp rather than friendly.
blueviolet: Blue Violet is the colour Prince might have specified for a stage cyc light - high chroma, deep enough to feel weighted, bright enough to throb under tungsten. The CSS hex was lifted from the X11 rgb.txt master file and has never been retuned. It carries enough visual mass to function as a heading colour or single accent, but flooding a large area with it tires the eye within a screen-height. Body text against white passes AA at standard sizes thanks to the depth. Pair it with a warm cream `#fdf6e3` for a Solarized-adjacent palette, or with mustard for poster work in the Bauhaus tradition.
bootstrap-danger: Bootstrap Danger is the red used for error alerts, destructive buttons, and validation warnings in Bootstrap 5. It is brighter and slightly cooler than the framework's red counterpart in the v4 release, tuned to maintain WCAG AA against white text at common button sizes. Because Bootstrap is still embedded in vast amounts of enterprise and admin software, this exact red has become a de facto convention for irreversible actions across the web. If your design system needs a destructive color, this hex is a defensible starting point. Pair with neutral grays for type so the red stays readable rather than alarming.
bootstrap-dark: Bootstrap Dark is the framework's near-black, used for the default navbar in dark mode and for emphasized text on light backgrounds. It is not pure black; the slight blue cast keeps it from feeling oppressive against neutral grays and softens edges in long blocks of text. The hex is shared across many shipped Bootstrap sites, so users have learned to read it as header chrome even without conscious recognition. Pair it with white or near-white for body text. A saturated accent color works for primary actions; avoid stacking two near-blacks of similar lightness, which loses hierarchy.
bootstrap-indigo: Bootstrap Indigo is a vibrant purple-blue used as an accent in the framework's extended palette. It is more saturated than the framework's primary blue and is typically reserved for marketing pages or specialty components rather than core UI chrome. The hex landed in Bootstrap 5 along with a refreshed accent set aimed at making default theming look less generic. Against white surfaces it carries enough contrast for buttons. For body text it is too thin and should be reserved for headings or links rather than long-form paragraphs, where eye strain becomes a problem within a page or two.
bootstrap-info: Bootstrap Info is the cyan-leaning blue used for non-critical informational alerts and badges. The framework picked a bright tone so that info messages would feel distinct from primary actions without competing with warnings or errors for attention. The color is too pale for white text at small sizes; Bootstrap's components rely on dark text overlays for the alert variants. If you are theming a system, treat this as a hint color rather than a primary, and reserve a deeper blue for actions that need to be obeyed. The semantic separation matters more than the exact hue.
bootstrap-light: Bootstrap Light is the framework's near-white surface color, used for backgrounds in card variants, footer bars, and table stripes. It sits warmer than pure white, which prevents the sterile-printer-paper effect that pure #ffffff can produce next to colored elements. The hex has become so widespread that a casual web user can subconsciously identify a Bootstrap-themed site by the specific off-white. Pair it with dark text and a single saturated accent. Use a slightly darker neutral for cards layered on top of it so the surface hierarchy stays readable without relying on shadows alone.
bootstrap-primary: Bootstrap Primary is the default action color across the framework: links, primary buttons, focused inputs, and active navigation items all default to this blue. It is calibrated to pass WCAG AA against white at standard button heights, which is why it tends to feel slightly darker than fashion-forward blues in newer design systems. Because billions of sessions have trained users to expect this exact hue as the thing to click, replacing it in a Bootstrap codebase requires updating user mental models as well as CSS variables. Treat it as a load-bearing convention rather than a stylistic choice.
bootstrap-secondary: Bootstrap Secondary is the framework's neutral mid-gray, used for de-emphasized buttons, muted text, and disabled-but-present UI states. It carries a faint cool tint that distinguishes it from warm grays in the same lightness band. The role is structural rather than expressive: it tells users this thing exists but is not the primary action. If you are reskinning a Bootstrap project, this is one of the colors you almost never need to change, because its semantic load is conventional rather than brand-specific. Body text against white passes AA at standard sizes but not AAA.
bootstrap-success: Bootstrap Success is the green used for confirmation alerts, completed-state badges, and primary call-to-action buttons in financial or onboarding flows. It is darker and more saturated than Material's success green, which gives it better contrast against white at small sizes but can feel slightly stiff in marketing contexts. The semantic load is universal across the framework's components, so users do not need a legend to know that a green Bootstrap badge means this thing worked. Use it for confirmations rather than for general decorative greens; the convention is strong enough that breaking it feels like a bug.
bootstrap-teal: Bootstrap Teal is a brighter, more saturated accent color introduced in Bootstrap 5's extended palette. It sits between Success green and Info cyan, and is typically used in marketing components, dashboards, and data visualization rather than core UI chrome. The hex reads as fresh against both white and dark surfaces, and works well for charts and graphs where it can carry data alongside other accent colors without competing for foreground attention. Pair with a deep navy or charcoal for type contrast. Body text contrast against white passes AA at headline sizes but is borderline below 14px.
bootstrap-warning: Bootstrap Warning is the framework's yellow, used for caution alerts, partial-state badges, and attention-required chips. It is bright enough that dark text on top is mandatory for accessibility; light text disappears entirely against it. The hex matches what most users have learned to read as caution but not danger across web interfaces. Use it for non-blocking warnings, validation hints, and visual flags that something needs review. For destructive or irreversible operations, use the red Danger color instead; mixing the two semantically confuses users who have learned to react differently to each.
brown: Brown is the CSS named color closest to the dictionary definition of the word: a deep, warm, reddish-tan. Despite the name, the hex sits in the red family by hue, just at low enough lightness to register as earthen rather than fiery. Brown shows up in heritage branding for coffee, leather goods, and outdoor equipment as the dominant tone. It pairs well with cream and brass accents, also with sage green for a vintage palette. On the web it is rare as a primary action color because it competes with red for attention without the same urgency cues, which can confuse users.
burlywood: Burlywood takes its name from the chunky, knotted outgrowths called burls that woodworkers prize for their unpredictable grain. The CSS value reads as raw pine after a season of sun: warm, slightly drained of saturation, more honest than honey. West Elm and Muji both use tones in this band as default surfaces in furniture catalogues because it suggests craft without naming a specific timber. Drop deep walnut `#3e2817` text on top and the layout starts to feel like a Kinfolk spread. The colour is too pale to function as type against white; reserve it for surfaces and hero washes that benefit from a warm fill.
burnt-orange: Burnt Orange is the rust-tinted orange of autumn leaves and 1970s upholstery. The exact value sits between true orange and red-brown, deep enough to feel earthy but bright enough to register as a hot color rather than a brown. It enjoyed a major revival in branding around 2018 when warm minimalism became fashionable in lifestyle and fashion. Use it as a primary accent against cream and charcoal. Pair with sage green for a vintage palette, or with deep teal for richer contrast. Reserve large areas for editorial layouts; in UI contexts it can be mistaken for a hover state on an orange button.
cadetblue: Cadet Blue is a desaturated blue-green, named after the dark teal of British naval cadet uniforms. The CSS hex is dustier than the historical dye, sitting in muted-teal territory. It is one of the rare CSS colors that ages well in modern flat-design palettes because its low chroma keeps it from looking dated. Use it as a secondary surface color for marine or heritage brands, or as a calming background for content-heavy pages where stronger blues would distract. Pair with cream or warm white for type contrast. Body text against white passes AA at headline sizes; verify smaller sizes carefully.
champagne: Champagne is a curated pale gold-yellow named after the wine. It is lighter and less saturated than true gold and reads as a sophisticated cream, common in luxury fashion, hospitality, and event branding. Use it as a background for invitations, wedding sites, or premium product pages, paired with deep burgundy type. On displays it can shift toward green under daylight color profiles, so verify the rendered color on both calibrated and uncalibrated screens before committing it to a brand. Two champagne surfaces of slightly different value can look like a calibration error rather than a tonal choice.
chartreuse: Chartreuse is the brilliant yellow-green named after the French herbal liqueur first distilled by Carthusian monks in the eighteenth century. The CSS value is exactly halfway between green and yellow at full saturation, which makes it one of the most aggressive colors a screen can produce. It signals warning in safety contexts and youth-energy in beverage or gaming branding. Reserve it for accent only; full-area chartreuse causes eye fatigue quickly and rarely passes accessibility checks against white. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size, so it must be used with dark text overlays whenever it appears as a surface.
chocolate: Chocolate sits in the brown family at moderate lightness with a strong red-orange undertone. The CSS name was chosen to mark this exact warm cocoa tone, distinct from cooler browns like Saddle Brown or pure Brown. Food and outdoor brands use it as a primary in autumn and holiday campaigns. Pair with cream for a hot-drink palette, or with brass and matte black for luxury packaging. As a UI color it works well for warm-themed buttons but loses contrast quickly on white. Body text against white passes AA at body-text sizes; below 14px it is borderline and should be checked carefully.
coral: Coral is the warm pink-orange named after the living reef organism. The CSS value is brighter than what most coral actually looks like underwater but matches the popular interior-design tone that took off in the 2000s and again in 2019 as Pantone's color of the year. Designers use it for energetic accent buttons, calls to action, and editorial palettes that want warmth without aggression. Against white the contrast is borderline for body text and reliable for headlines. Pair it with deep navy or charcoal for type-heavy layouts; the contrast between the cool dark type and warm coral surface reads as intentional rather than accidental.
cornflowerblue: Cornflower Blue is the colour of the European cornflower, Centaurea cyanus, and famously of the bottle Bombay Sapphire chose in 1986 to differentiate itself from clear-glass competitors. On the web it carries a 2000s nostalgia - early Blogger themes shipped it as a default link colour - but the hue holds up. Use it as a friendly secondary accent against charcoal `#1f2937` text or as a hero wash beneath cream copy. Contrast against white sits just below AA at body sizes, so reserve it for headlines 18px and above or large UI elements. Pair it with terracotta for a Mediterranean palette that avoids the obvious navy-and-orange cliché.
cornsilk: Cornsilk is the pale, dry yellow of dried corn husks, almost off-white. It is one of CSS's quieter cream tones, sitting between Floral White and Lemon Chiffon. Stationery, food-blog, and craft-product designs use it as a soft background that warms a page without committing to a real yellow accent. It pairs with browns and deep greens for harvest palettes, or with charcoal for type. Against pure white it reads as faintly yellow; against a yellow accent it can disappear into the background. Layer it as a single-surface tone rather than stacking it against other pale yellows, where the distinction gets lost.
crimson: Crimson is a deep, slightly blue-leaning red with strong cultural weight, historically associated with royalty, academia, and ceremony. The CSS value is darker and richer than pure red, which makes it a more legible choice for buttons and links because contrast against white holds up at small sizes. Use it as a primary action color where the brand needs gravitas rather than urgency. It pairs well with cream, deep navy, and warm gold, and reads especially well in long-form editorial layouts. Body text in Crimson against white passes AA comfortably at every size and is one of the more accessible saturated reds in the spec.
cyan: Cyan is one of the four CMYK process inks that built modern offset printing, and on a calibrated sRGB display the pure value is the most saturated cyan the gamut can render. It hums. Twitch and the Brazilian flag both lean on tones in this range when they need to feel digital rather than natural. The CSS alias `aqua` resolves to the same hex; Microsoft coined `aqua` for the original Windows 3.1 palette, while the print industry kept the older Greek-derived term. Body text against white falls below AA at every size, so park the full-saturation value behind dark labels in dashboard tiles rather than asking it to carry copy.
darkblue: Dark Blue in CSS predates the spec by decades; it came in through the original X11 rgb.txt list assembled at MIT in the 1980s and has barely moved since. The hex is a shade darker than CSS Navy and reads as the kind of authoritative blue a 1996 IBM annual report would have specified for heading typography. Body text in Dark Blue against white passes AAA at every size, which makes it one of the very few CSS named colours that work confidently as long-form copy. Pair it with a warm cream `#fef3c7` or with brushed brass for a heritage palette that nods to Penguin Classics.
darkcyan: Dark Cyan is the deep blue-green of mineral pools and oxidized copper. The CSS hex sits at low-to-mid lightness with full saturation, which gives it strong presence against pale backgrounds. It enjoys steady use in environmental, scientific, and medical branding where the calmer cousin of teal feels too pale. Pair it with cream and warm wood tones for a natural palette, or with bright coral for high-contrast editorial layouts. On screens it can read as black on some dim displays; check rendering before committing. Body text contrast against white passes AAA at standard sizes.
darkgoldenrod: Dark Goldenrod is a deep mustard-yellow with brown undertones, named after the late-summer wildflower. The CSS hex is darker and richer than the actual flower, sitting closer to bronze. It works as a heritage accent in food, beverage, and outdoor brands where its warmth carries connotations of harvest and craft. Pair with deep green or burgundy for a vintage palette. Against white the contrast is adequate for headlines but borderline for body text; reserve it for display use. The closest analog in modern systems is Tailwind Amber 600 or Yellow 700, both of which sit at similar perceived lightness.
darkgray: Dark Gray in CSS is, confusingly, lighter than CSS Gray. The X11 palette swapped the lightness scale at some point and the spec inherited the result, so a designer expecting a deeper neutral may be surprised. Despite the misnomer, the hex is a useful mid-light gray for secondary UI text, borders, and disabled states. It has enough warmth in its undertone to feel less clinical than pure neutral grays. Pair with deep charcoal for primary text and an off-white surface. Body text against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve it for secondary roles where reduced legibility is intentional.
darkgreen: Dark Green is the CSS value closest to the green of a healthy forest canopy under daylight. It carries strong heritage, conservation, and finance associations across cultures. The hex is dark enough to function as type against pale backgrounds and as a primary brand color for environmentally-positioned products. Pair with cream and warm beige for a natural palette, or with bright coral for a high-contrast pairing. Avoid placing it directly next to pure red, which produces strong visual vibration that fatigues the eye. Body text against white passes AAA at all standard sizes, so it works for long-form display use.
darkkhaki: Dark Khaki is a muted yellow-brown named after the dust-toned uniforms of late nineteenth century British forces in South Asia. The CSS hex is desaturated and slightly olive, distinct from the lighter Khaki entry. Outdoor gear and earthy interior palettes use it as a primary or secondary tone. Pair with deep navy for type contrast, or with cream and warm wood for a calmer outdoor palette. The low chroma makes it easy on the eye for large-area surfaces; long-form reading against a Dark Khaki background feels less fatiguing than against pure white under tungsten light at evening hours.
darkmagenta: Dark Magenta is a deep, slightly red-leaning purple, sitting between true purple and magenta. The CSS hex is rich enough for legible button states and small text against white. Cultural associations are mixed: religious, theatrical, and luxurious depending on context. Use it as a brand accent for cosmetics, premium services, or arts organizations. It pairs well with chartreuse for complementary tension, or with deep cream for an editorial palette. Reserve full-area use for hero sections; over large UI surfaces it feels heavy and can compete with the type for foreground attention. Body text against white passes AAA at standard sizes.
darkolivegreen: Dark Olive Green is the deep gray-green of preserved olives and military field jackets. The CSS hex is desaturated and earthy, distinct from the brighter Olive Drab. Outdoor brands and natural-product packaging use it as a primary or grounding color. Pair with cream and brass for a heritage palette, or with bright coral for unexpected warmth. The low chroma makes it suitable for large surfaces without fatigue and works well as a background for cream-colored type. Body text against white passes AAA at all standard sizes; the chroma is low enough that adjacent text rarely vibrates.
darkorange: Dark Orange is the saturated, slightly red-leaning orange of late autumn pumpkins. The CSS hex is brighter than burnt orange and more saturated than CSS Orange, carrying high energy without tipping into red. It is a strong call-to-action color for energetic brands in sports, e-commerce, and food delivery. Pair with deep navy or charcoal for type contrast; pairing with red creates visual confusion. Against white it passes contrast for headlines but should be checked at body-text sizes. Body text against white in Dark Orange falls below AA at sizes under 18px and should be reserved for emphasis.
darkorchid: Dark Orchid is a vivid mid-purple named after the bloom rather than the leaf. The CSS hex has high chroma at mid-low lightness and reads as theatrical purple. Cosmetics, music, and arts branding use it as a primary accent. Pair with deep teal or gold for a luxury palette, or with pale cream for a softer contrast. It is too saturated for large surfaces but works well as a button color, link state, or display heading on white backgrounds. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard sizes; the saturation level can feel intense in long blocks of text and is best for headlines.
darkred: Dark Red is the deep, slightly brown-leaning red of dried blood and aged wine. The CSS hex is one of the most legible red shades on white because the depth pushes it firmly above AA contrast thresholds for normal text. It is a workhorse for financial, automotive, and luxury brands where seriousness matters more than energy. Pair with cream for editorial palettes, or with deep navy for a richer tonal stack. Use sparingly: large fields of dark red read as heavy and can feel ominous in unintended contexts. Body text against white passes AAA at all standard sizes.
darksalmon: Dark Salmon is a muted pink-orange named after the slightly darker flesh of fresh-caught Atlantic salmon. The CSS hex sits between Salmon and Peach Puff, with low chroma and warm undertones. Beauty, wellness, and food brands use it as a soft accent that suggests warmth without bright energy. Pair with deep teal for complementary contrast, or with charcoal for type. Avoid pairing with bright reds, which will pull the warmth out and make it look muddy by comparison. Body text against white falls below AA at every size; reserve it for backgrounds and accents.
darkseagreen: Dark Sea Green is a muted, dusty mid-green named after the deeper shade of seaweed. The CSS hex has low chroma and a slight blue cast, distinct from the warmer mid-greens in the spec. It enjoys steady use in wellness, organic-food, and home-decor branding where a calmer green is preferred to brighter alternatives. Pair with cream and terracotta for a natural palette, or with off-white and charcoal for a modern minimalist look. It works well as a large surface tone without fatiguing the eye. Body text against white passes AA at headline sizes but is borderline below 14px.
darkslateblue: Dark Slate Blue is a deep, slightly violet-leaning blue-gray with strong heritage associations. The CSS hex is dark enough to function as type against pale backgrounds and works well as a primary brand color for financial, legal, or editorial products. It pairs well with cream and warm gold for an editorial palette, or with rust accents for a heritage look. Against white the contrast holds at small sizes. Against off-white it produces a calm library-shelf feel that suits long-form reading layouts. Body text against white passes AAA at every size, making it one of the more accessible blue-purples in the spec.
darkslategray: Dark Slate Gray is a deep, slightly green-blue charcoal. The CSS hex is darker than most modern UI grays and has enough chroma to feel intentional rather than neutral. Architecture, interior, and editorial design use it as a sophisticated alternative to pure charcoal because the cool undertone reads as considered rather than default. Pair with cream and warm wood for interior palettes, or with bright coral for high-contrast layouts. As body text on white it passes AAA comfortably. The chroma is low enough that adjacent saturated accent colors will not produce visual vibration against it.
darkturquoise: Dark Turquoise is the saturated mid-cyan of swimming-pool tiles and turquoise jewelry. The CSS hex is brighter than the actual mineral, sitting closer to a fluorescent aqua. Travel, beauty, and wellness brands use it as a fresh primary; it carries strong tropical and aquatic associations across cultures. Pair with cream for a beach palette, or with deep navy for tonal depth. Reserve large-area use for hero sections; full-saturation turquoise can vibrate against white at smaller sizes. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at every size; the value works as a surface or button color only, not as text.
darkviolet: Dark Violet is a saturated mid-purple with strong red-leaning undertones, distinct from the cooler Blue Violet. The CSS hex carries enough chroma to function as a primary accent against white at headline sizes. Cosmetic, arts, and premium-service branding use it for visual emphasis. Pair with chartreuse for complementary tension, or with deep gold for luxury contexts. Reserve body-text application for larger sizes; at small sizes the contrast against white is on the borderline. Body text contrast against white passes AA at sizes 16px and above, just barely below AAA at body sizes.
deeppink: Deep Pink is the high-chroma magenta-pink that powered early 2000s pop branding and shows no sign of fading. The CSS hex is one of the most saturated pink values an sRGB display can produce. Use it for energetic accent only; large fields of Deep Pink fatigue the eye within seconds. It pairs well with deep teal for a 90s pop palette, or with cream for a romantic editorial look. Against white the contrast is sufficient for headlines but the saturation can feel aggressive at body text sizes. Body text contrast against white passes AA at headline sizes; below 14px it is borderline.
deepskyblue: Deep Sky Blue is the saturated cyan-blue of a cloudless midday sky at altitude. The CSS hex carries strong association with technology, communications, and travel brands. It is bright enough to feel energetic without veering into childish primary blue. Pair with cream and warm coral for an open-air palette, or with deep navy for tonal depth. At full saturation it reads as a primary accent against white. The contrast for body text is borderline at small sizes, so reserve it for headlines, buttons, and decorative elements rather than long paragraphs. Body text against white falls below AA at body sizes.
dimgray: Dim Gray is a mid-charcoal that sits between true gray and dark charcoal. The CSS hex is calibrated to feel low-energy without disappearing into black, which makes it a steady choice for secondary type, borders, and de-emphasized labels in interface design. The lack of color cast keeps it neutral against any accent color. Against white it passes AAA contrast comfortably; against light gray surfaces it remains readable but loses some impact, so consider stepping down to a darker shade if needed. Body text on Dim Gray surfaces should use white or near-white type to maintain legible contrast.
dodgerblue: Dodger Blue is a bright mid-blue named after the baseball franchise rather than the artful pedestrian. The CSS hex sits between Sky Blue and Royal Blue, vivid enough to feel modern but desaturated enough to avoid the dated feel of pure web-safe blue. It became one of the standard primary action colors of the 2010s, appearing in early Twitter, Trello, and a long list of SaaS dashboards. Pair with charcoal type and cream surfaces for a friendly tech palette. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve text use for headlines and rely on dark text overlays for button surfaces.
dusty-rose: Dusty Rose is a curated muted pink with strong brown undertones, popular in fashion, hospitality, and wedding branding. The hex sits in the warm-neutral zone of the color wheel and reads as romantic without leaning juvenile. Pair with sage green for a vintage palette, with cream and brass for luxury hospitality, or with deep burgundy for an editorial fashion contrast. As a primary brand color it works best in lifestyle contexts; in business or technical interfaces it can feel out of register against the conventional palette. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; treat it as a surface rather than a type color.
firebrick: Firebrick is the deep, slightly brown-leaning red of weathered house bricks. The CSS hex has high chroma at low-mid lightness, giving it strong presence as a primary accent or display heading color. It carries warm, grounded, heritage associations: architecture, leather goods, and serious editorial design. Pair with cream and dark charcoal for a heritage palette, or with deep teal for a striking contrast. Against white the contrast holds at body text sizes, making it one of the more accessible reds in the CSS spec. Body text against white passes AAA at standard sizes; smaller-than-12px should still be verified per browser.
floralwhite: Floral White is one of the warmest off-whites in the CSS spec, sitting closer to ivory than to pure white. The name refers to the cream color of common garden flowers, and the hex carries a faint yellow undertone that makes it feel inviting rather than clinical. Stationery, beauty, and wedding designers use it as a background that softens dark text without committing to a real cream. Pair with deep brown for type, or with muted forest green for a softer secondary accent. The warm cast makes long-form reading less fatiguing than pure white under tungsten light at evening hours.
forestgreen: Forest Green is the deep, slightly cool green of pine and fir canopy seen in late afternoon. The CSS hex is darker than most digital greens and is one of the more legible greens against white at body text sizes. Heritage outdoor brands, conservation groups, and editorial design use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream and brass for a classic outdoor palette, or with terracotta for an unexpected complementary accent. It works well as a UI accent for environmental or finance products. Body text against white passes AAA at all standard sizes, so the value carries long-form use confidently.
gainsboro: Gainsboro is the very light gray named after a town in Lincolnshire, England, by way of the eighteenth-century painter Thomas Gainsborough. The CSS hex is a quiet, balanced gray with no obvious cool or warm cast, which makes it a useful neutral for borders, dividers, and disabled-state surfaces in interface design. Against white it provides just enough separation to read as a distinct layer without competing for attention. Pair with mid-gray text and a single saturated accent. Body text against Gainsboro surfaces should use a deep charcoal or near-black to maintain legible contrast at all sizes.
ghostwhite: Ghost White is the slightly blue-tinted off-white that suggests cold light without committing to a real blue. The CSS hex sits one degree away from white, distinguishable mainly when placed next to true white in adjacent surfaces. Tech, financial, and dashboard designs use it as a background that reads as clean without the harshness of pure white. Pair with charcoal or near-black for type, and reserve real color accents for buttons, badges, and emphasized text. Two Ghost White surfaces side by side will appear identical in dim viewing; treat it as a single-layer surface rather than for nested cards.
gold: Gold in CSS is the bright, saturated yellow of metallic gold seen in direct sunlight rather than the deeper bronze of jewelry. The hex has very high chroma and lightness, which means it cannot be used reliably as text against white because the contrast fails accessibility checks. Reserve it for background fills, badges, and decorative accents. It pairs well with deep navy or burgundy for heritage palettes, or with matte black for luxury branding. Avoid pairing with yellow-green, which produces visual conflict. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; gold must be used with dark text overlays on surfaces.
goldenrod: Solidago, the goldenrod, blankets late-summer roadsides across North America in exactly the kind of warm mustard the CSS hex tries to capture. The web value is more saturated than the actual wildflower but reads cleanly as harvest yellow. Editorial brands such as Field Notes use tones in this band for limited-edition covers because the warmth signals craft. Drop charcoal `#262626` type on a Goldenrod field and the contrast holds at AAA for headings, AA only at body sizes 18px and above. Pair it with deep teal for the kind of complementary tension Saul Bass leaned on in his late-period film posters; pure black flattens the warmth and should be avoided.
gray: Gray in CSS is the dead-center neutral at #808080, the midpoint of the RGB cube. It is the textbook medium gray but rarely appears in modern design systems because purpose-built neutrals with warm or cool casts tend to feel more intentional. Use it sparingly as a stand-in for true neutral in code samples or technical documentation. Most UI designs prefer a slightly cooler or warmer mid-gray for type and borders. Body text in CSS Gray against white passes AA at sizes 18px and above but fails at body text sizes; for type, step down to a darker gray like Dim Gray or #4a5565.
green: CSS Green is `#008000`, a value chosen in 1996 because it sat exactly halfway between the brightest sRGB green and black on each channel. It is rarely what designers want when they say green - modern palettes reach for the brighter Tailwind `#22c55e` or Material Green 500 instead - but the keyword still carries the historical weight. The colour reads as serious rather than fresh. Pair it with cream and warm oak for an outdoor palette, or with charcoal `#0a0a0a` for a hospital-grade clinical look. Body text against white passes AAA at sizes 16px and above, which makes it usable for long-form layouts that need a green hierarchy colour.
greenyellow: Green Yellow is the safety-vest hue construction crews and emergency dispatchers reach for when they need a colour the eye cannot ignore. The CSS value sits in the same band as 3M's Diamond Grade reflective tape. Use it strictly as an accent - a tag pill, a focus ring, a warning state - never as a surface. It cannot function as body text against any white background, falling well below AA at every size. Pair it with a deep ink like `#0a0a0a` for type, or use it as a single splash against muted forest green to amplify the contrast without the surrounding palette tipping into a sports-team aesthetic.
honeydew: Honeydew is a pale, slightly green-tinted off-white named after the melon. The CSS hex is one of the quieter pastels in the spec and works well as a calm background for wellness, food, and skincare brands. It pairs with deep green for type, or with terracotta for a softer secondary accent. Two Honeydew surfaces sitting adjacent to each other will read as identical in dim viewing conditions, so use it as a single-layer background rather than for layered surfaces. The cool tint distinguishes it from warm off-whites like Floral White or Seashell, which carry yellow undertones instead.
hotpink: Hot Pink is the bright, saturated pink that dominated 1980s pop fashion and resurfaces in cosmetic, music, and youth-targeted branding. The CSS hex is one of the most attention-grabbing colors in the spec, with high chroma at moderate lightness. Use it as an accent rather than a body color; large fields of Hot Pink fatigue the eye and can read as juvenile. Pair with deep navy for type, with cream for a softer editorial palette, or with chartreuse for a high-energy complementary clash. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; reserve text use for emphasis.
indianred: Indian Red is a muted, brick-toned red that takes its name from a historic pigment derived from iron-rich earth in South Asia, not from any depiction of people. The CSS hex sits at mid-lightness with moderate chroma, less aggressive than full red and more inviting than dark red. Heritage and craft brands use it as a primary accent. Pair with cream for a warmer palette, or with deep teal for unexpected contrast. Against white it carries enough contrast for buttons and headlines. Body text contrast against white passes AA at sizes 18px and above; below that it falls below the threshold.
indigo: Indigo names a deep blue-violet historically derived from the indigofera plant, one of the oldest natural dyes still in use. The CSS hex is darker than most digital indigos and reads as a near-purple navy. Cultural associations are wide-ranging: textiles, denim, philosophy, music, the seventh color of the spectrum. Use it as a primary brand color for editorial, fashion, or contemplative products. Pair with warm cream and brass for heritage palettes, or with deep red for an unexpected accent. Body text against white passes AAA at all standard sizes, making it a confident text color for long-form layouts.
ivory: Ivory takes its name from elephant tusk; the modern colour mostly lives on as paint chips at Benjamin Moore and as the lining of wedding invitations. The CSS hex carries a barely-there yellow undertone that nudges it warmer than Floral White and saves it from clinical glare. Stationery brands like Crane & Co. have used tones in this band for over a century because the warmth flatters letterpress ink. Use Ivory as a primary background paired with charcoal `#1c1917` body type. The warm cast reduces evening eye strain compared to pure white, which is why reader-mode toggles in Pocket default to off-whites within a few delta-E units of this hex.
khaki: British troops dyed their cotton uniforms with native earth in 1840s Punjab and called the result khaki, from the Urdu for dust. The CSS value drifted toward yellow over the decades of monitor-spec lists and now sits more banana than burlap, but the name still anchors it to its military origin. Patagonia and Filson both use surfaces in this band for catalogue washes because it reads as outdoors without specifying a biome. Body text in Khaki against white fails AA at every size - treat it strictly as a surface colour with dark type overlays. Pair it with deep navy `#1e3a8a` body copy for a 1940s travel-poster feel.
lavender: True lavender oil is purple-pink and floral; CSS Lavender is a near-white violet that the X11 graphics committee picked from a Pantone-style sample book in 1985 and that has never been retuned. The value reads as quiet rather than aromatic. Wellness brands like Caraway and Hims lean on tones in this band for packaging because the cool undertone signals calm without committing to gendered pink. Use it as a page wash beneath aubergine `#581c87` headings or as a card background against charcoal body copy. Place it next to bright yellow and the violet cast amplifies into something almost garish - a warning the X11 committee never bothered to document.
lavenderblush: Lavender Blush is one of the most obscure CSS named colors, a very pale pink with a faint violet undertone. The hex reads as nearly white in normal viewing and is rarely chosen as an intentional brand color. It works as a quiet background for beauty product photography or wedding stationery where a near-white with a barely-visible warmth is wanted. Pair with deep charcoal or muted burgundy for type. Against pure white it loses all distinction and reads as a calibration discrepancy rather than a real surface, so it should never be placed adjacent to true white in a layout.
lawngreen: Lawn Green is the saturated, slightly yellow-leaning green of fresh turf in spring. The CSS hex is one of the brightest greens in the spec, almost neon, and is too saturated for most UI applications. Use it as a safety or marker color in maps and dashboards, or as a high-energy accent in sports and gaming branding. Against white the contrast fails at body text sizes; reserve it for backgrounds, fills, and decorative elements with dark text on top. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size, so the value cannot function as a type color.
lemonchiffon: Lemon Chiffon is a pale, slightly green-yellow cream named after the airy citrus dessert. The CSS hex is one of the gentler yellows in the spec and works as a warm background for food, beauty, and editorial design. Pair with deep brown for type, or with warm orange for accent contrast. Against pure white it reads as faintly yellow; against a saturated yellow accent it disappears into the background, so layer thoughtfully. Two Lemon Chiffon surfaces in adjacent layers will produce a subtle but visible boundary that can read as banding rather than as separation.
lightblue: Light Blue in CSS is a pale, slightly cyan-leaning sky blue. The hex is lighter and a touch cooler than Sky Blue, with low chroma that makes it suitable for large-area surfaces without visual fatigue. Wellness, baby goods, and tech-product branding use it as a calming background. Pair with deep navy for type, with white for layered surfaces, and with warm coral for unexpected accent contrast. Against pure white it provides enough separation to read as a distinct layer for product cards or panels. Body text against Light Blue should use a deep charcoal or near-black for legible contrast.
lightcoral: Light Coral is the softer, paler version of Coral, with the same warm pink-orange hue at higher lightness. The CSS hex reads as a gentle, slightly muted pink and works in beauty, wellness, and editorial palettes where energy without aggression is wanted. Pair with cream for a softer look, or with deep teal for a richer contrast. Against white it does not carry enough contrast for body text; reserve it for backgrounds, headlines, and accent buttons with appropriate dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size below 18px and is borderline at headline sizes.
lightcyan: Light Cyan is the palest member of the cyan family, a very faint blue-green that reads as nearly off-white. The CSS hex is rarely chosen as an intentional brand color because the saturation is too low to register as a real cyan at typical viewing distances. It works as a quiet background for medical, dental, and clinical branding where a hint of cool fresh feel is wanted. Pair with deep teal or charcoal for type. Two Light Cyan surfaces side by side will look identical in normal viewing; treat the color as a single-layer surface tone rather than for nested cards.
lightgoldenrodyellow: Light Goldenrod Yellow is one of the most awkwardly named CSS colors and one of the palest. The hex is a faint cream with a yellow undertone, slightly lighter than Lemon Chiffon. Food, beauty, and stationery designs use it as a warm background that suggests sunlight without committing to a real yellow. Pair with deep brown or warm charcoal for type. Against pure white the difference is subtle; use it in layered surfaces rather than as a primary background. Body text against this surface should use a deep neutral; light type disappears completely against the warm pale cast.
lightgray: Light Gray in CSS is a pale neutral useful for dividers, disabled-state surfaces, and quiet backgrounds. The hex has no obvious warm or cool cast, which keeps it versatile across mixed-temperature palettes. It pairs with any darker neutral or saturated accent. Against white it provides enough separation for borders and quiet surfaces; against mid-gray text it carries decent but not strong contrast, so verify accessibility for secondary text use. Body text on a Light Gray surface should use deep charcoal or near-black to maintain legible AA contrast at body sizes.
lightgreen: Light Green is a pale, slightly yellow-leaning green that reads as fresh and friendly. The CSS hex is lighter than Pale Green and works as a primary surface for wellness, food, and outdoor brands. Pair with deep green for type, with cream for a softer palette, or with terracotta for an unexpected warmth. Against white it provides reasonable separation; against pure green it loses distinction quickly. As a UI accent it works well for success states with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; use it as a surface only.
lightpink: Light Pink is the pale, slightly warm pink of, originally, baby clothes. The CSS hex reads as gentle, romantic, and slightly nostalgic. Beauty, wellness, and stationery designs use it as a background or secondary surface. Pair with deep burgundy for type, or with muted teal for a calmer secondary accent. Avoid pairing it with hot pink, which makes the lightness shift jump uncomfortably and the layout starts to feel like a gradient went wrong. Against white it provides enough separation to read as a distinct layer for product cards or panels in a layered UI.
lightsalmon: Light Salmon is a pale, warm pink-orange that sits between Peach Puff and Salmon. The CSS hex reads as a gentle, sunlit pink and works in beauty, wellness, and editorial palettes. Pair with cream for a quieter palette, or with deep teal for unexpected contrast. It is too pale to function as text against white but works well as a background or large surface color. The warm undertone keeps it from feeling clinical against neutral grays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; reserve it as a surface tone with dark text overlays.
lightseagreen: Light Sea Green is a mid-saturation teal that sits between Sea Green and Turquoise. The CSS hex carries enough chroma to function as a primary brand color while staying calm enough for large-area use. Travel, wellness, and environmental brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for a tropical palette, or with deep navy for tonal contrast. Against white it passes contrast for headlines and large buttons but is borderline for small body text. Body text against white passes AA at sizes 18px and above; below that, step down to a darker shade for body text use.
lightskyblue: Light Sky Blue is a pale, slightly cyan-leaning sky blue, lighter and more saturated than Light Blue. The CSS hex reads as fresh and open, common in travel, weather, and aviation branding. Pair with deep navy for type, with cream for a softer palette, or with warm coral for accent. Against white it provides reasonable separation but is too pale for body text. Reserve large-area use for hero sections and panel backgrounds; in dashboards and content areas the saturation level can fight with adjacent data visualization colors that share the same lightness band.
lightslategray: Light Slate Gray is a mid-light blue-gray with enough chroma to read as cool rather than fully neutral. The CSS hex sits in the territory of modern UI mid-grays used for secondary text and borders. Editorial, business, and tech designs use it for de-emphasized text or panel backgrounds. Pair with charcoal for primary text and a single warm accent. Against white it carries enough contrast for secondary text at body sizes but should be checked for AA compliance. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard body sizes; AAA holds only at headline sizes.
lightsteelblue: Light Steel Blue is a pale, slightly violet-leaning blue-gray that reads as restrained and slightly nostalgic. The CSS hex carries low chroma at high lightness, making it useful as a calm background for editorial, financial, or heritage brands. Pair with deep navy for type, with cream for a quieter palette, or with brass for a heritage accent. It works well as a background for long-form reading or as a panel color in dashboards. Two Light Steel Blue surfaces adjacent to each other will read as a single surface in dim viewing; layer carefully.
lightyellow: Light Yellow is a very pale, faintly green-yellow cream. The CSS hex reads as warm but barely chromatic, useful as a background for food, beauty, or wellness designs where a hint of sunlight is wanted. Pair with deep brown or charcoal for type. Against pure white the difference is subtle, so it works best as a layered surface or panel background rather than as a primary page color. Avoid placing it next to brighter yellows, which will make Light Yellow look gray by comparison and create the impression of a calibration shift rather than a deliberate layered palette.
lime: Lime in CSS is #00ff00, the brightest possible green the sRGB color space can produce. It is too saturated for almost any practical UI application and is most often used as a debugging stand-in or as a high-contrast marker in technical diagrams. Cultural associations are split: tropical fruit, neon signage, gaming, and energy drinks. Real-world lime branding usually pulls a slightly desaturated variant. Pair with deep charcoal or magenta for high-contrast pop palettes. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; the value works only as a surface or marker.
limegreen: Lime Green is the colour Sprite added to its global rebrand in 2008, replacing the more muted bottle-green that Coca-Cola had used since the 1960s. The CSS hex carries enough saturation to feel young and slightly artificial. It works well as a CTA or success state in modern UI but tires the eye quickly when used across large surfaces. Pair it with a deep ink `#0a0a0a` for type and reserve it for the moment in the layout where the eye should land. Body text in Lime Green against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; for smaller copy step down to a deeper Tailwind Green 700 or similar.
linen: Linen is the warm off-white of unbleached natural fiber. The CSS hex carries a slightly pink-yellow undertone that reads as artisanal and tactile, distinct from cooler whites. Editorial, hospitality, and craft-product brands use it as a primary background. Pair with deep brown for type, or with terracotta for a secondary accent. The warm cast makes it especially comfortable for long-form reading and for product photography where a hint of texture without competing color is wanted. The undertone means cool grays placed adjacent to Linen will read as slightly off rather than as considered neutrals.
magenta: Magenta is one of the four CMYK process inks and one of the most saturated colors a screen can produce. The CSS hex is identical to Fuchsia at #ff00ff, an artifact of two separate HTML and X11 color lists merging. Cultural associations are pop, theater, drag, and tech-industry rebrand attempts. Use it strictly as an accent; large fields of magenta fatigue the eye in seconds. Pair with deep teal or charcoal for high-contrast pairings. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; reserve the full-saturation value for surfaces, badges, and dark-text overlays only.
maroon: Maroon entered English from the French marron, meaning chestnut, and lived for centuries as the heraldic colour of Welsh rugby and Texas A&M before HTML 3.2 standardised the hex in 1997. The CSS value is low-lightness brick red - closer to dried wine than to fresh fruit. Heritage brands in fashion and academia have leaned on it because the depth reads as serious without going corporate. Pair it with a warm cream `#fef3c7` for a Penguin Classics feel, or with brushed brass for editorial. Body text against white passes AAA at every standard size, making Maroon one of the few CSS spec colours that holds up confidently as long-form type.
material-blue-500: Material Blue 500 is the hex Google's design team selected in 2014 as the default accent for Android Lollipop, the release that introduced Material Design. The value was tuned for legibility against white at button sizes rather than for body type, and the friendly mid-saturation became the dominant blue of 2010s app design - used in everything from Trello to early Google Workspace logos. In Material-themed projects it remains the canonical primary action colour. Outside that context it can feel dated against newer OKLCH-tuned blues. Body text against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve text use for buttons and headlines 18px and above.
material-cyan-500: Material Cyan 500 is the bright teal-blue used as an accent color in Material Design 2. The hex carries enough chroma to function as a primary or strong secondary accent. It is one of the friendlier cyans in popular design systems, neither too pale nor too saturated for typical UI use. Use it for primary buttons in Material-themed designs, or as a secondary accent in palettes that need a bright cool tone. Against white it passes contrast for headlines but is borderline for body text. Body text contrast against white falls below AA below 18px size.
material-deep-purple-500: Material Deep Purple 500 is the rich, slightly blue-leaning purple in Material Design 2's expanded palette. The hex carries strong chroma at mid-low lightness and reads as confident and slightly theatrical. Use it as a primary accent for Material-themed projects, or as a secondary color for cosmetic, music, or arts branding. Pair with cream and warm brass for luxury palettes, or with chartreuse for complementary tension. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably. Material's Deep Purple was retired from the M3 baseline palette but remains a heavily-used hex across legacy Android apps.
material-green-500: Material Green 500 is the warm mid-green used for success states, completed badges, and primary positive actions in Material Design 2. The hex reads as friendly and natural rather than clinical. Use it as a confirmation color in Material-themed projects. Pair with deep gray for type and a single saturated accent for primary actions. Against white it passes contrast for headlines and large buttons but is borderline for body text; reserve text use for larger sizes. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; below that, the value should appear with dark text overlays.
material-indigo-500: Material Indigo 500 is the deep blue-purple Google chose as the original Material Theme default in 2014. It became one of the defining colors of mobile app design for several years. The hex is darker and less saturated than Material Blue 500, with a slight violet shift that makes it feel more sophisticated. Use it as a primary accent in heritage Material projects or in modern designs where a deeper, calmer blue is wanted. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with warm coral for unexpected accent. Body text contrast against white passes AAA at standard body sizes.
material-light-blue-500: Material Light Blue 500 is a brighter, slightly cyan-leaning blue from Material Design 2's expanded palette. The hex reads as fresh and informational, well suited to system alerts, hint states, and friendly call-to-action buttons. Pair with deep navy or charcoal for type contrast. As a primary brand color it works for travel, communications, and wellness products. Against white it passes contrast for headlines and large buttons; check body text use against AA thresholds. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve text use for sizes 18px and above.
material-pink-500: Material Pink 500 is the strong, slightly red-leaning pink in Material Design 2's primary palette. The hex carries enough chroma to function as a primary brand color or strong accent. It became one of the friendlier pinks in popular design systems, neither pastel nor neon, which is why it has appeared in countless health, beauty, and dating apps over the past decade. Use it for cosmetic, fashion, and youth-oriented products. Pair with deep navy for type contrast, or with cream for a softer palette. Body text against white passes AA at headline sizes only.
material-purple-500: Material Purple 500 is the saturated mid-purple in Material Design 2's primary palette. The hex sits between Material Pink and Material Deep Purple and reads as confident without being theatrical. Use it as a primary accent in Material-themed projects, or as a secondary color for cosmetic, music, or premium services. Pair with deep gray for type, or with gold for a luxury contrast. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines and buttons. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard body sizes; the saturation level can feel intense in long blocks of running text.
material-red-500: Material Red 500 is the primary destructive and error color in Material Design 2. The hex was tuned to pass WCAG AA against white text at standard button sizes while still feeling urgent. It became the conventional red for irreversible action buttons in 2010s app design. Use it for destructive actions, error states, and validation messages in Material-themed projects. For non-Material designs it can feel slightly orange-leaning compared to newer OKLCH-tuned reds. Body text contrast against white passes AA at body sizes; AAA holds at headline sizes only.
material-teal-500: Material Teal 500 is the deep, slightly blue-leaning teal in Material Design 2's primary palette. The hex carries strong chroma at mid-low lightness and was one of the framework's signature accents in early Android releases. Use it as a primary brand color for finance, technology, or wellness products. Pair with cream and warm coral for a fresh palette, or with charcoal for a clean professional look. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably at most sizes. Body text in Material Teal against white passes AA at standard body sizes and AAA at headline sizes.
mediumaquamarine: Medium Aquamarine is the muted mid-saturation cousin of Aquamarine, sitting in the soft-mint zone of the color wheel. The CSS hex reads as calm and slightly nostalgic, with low enough chroma to work as a primary surface for wellness, beauty, and editorial brands. Pair with cream for a soft palette, or with deep teal for tonal depth. Against white it provides reasonable separation as a panel background but is too pale for body text. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; treat it as a surface tone with dark text overlays rather than as a type color.
mediumblue: Medium Blue is a deep, fully saturated blue that sits between pure CSS Blue and Dark Blue. The hex reads as confident and authoritative, useful for editorial, financial, and government-themed branding. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm gold for a heritage palette. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably and works well as a primary action color. The depth and saturation give it more visual weight than mid-bright blues, so use it for emphasis rather than ambient surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AAA at standard sizes; the saturation can still feel intense in long-form text.
mediumorchid: Medium Orchid is a saturated mid-purple with a slight pink lean, named after the flower. The CSS hex carries high chroma at mid lightness, vivid enough to function as a primary accent. Cosmetic, fashion, and arts brands use it for emphasis. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with deep teal for a richer contrast. It is too saturated for body text against white but works well as a button color, link state, or display heading. Reserve large-area use for hero sections; in long-form layouts the chroma fights with the type for foreground attention.
mediumpurple: Medium Purple is a softer, slightly blue-leaning purple at moderate lightness and chroma. The CSS hex reads as restrained and slightly thoughtful, distinct from the more theatrical Medium Orchid. Editorial, educational, and contemplative brands use it as a primary or secondary accent. Pair with cream and warm gold for heritage palettes, or with deep teal for tonal contrast. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines and large buttons but is borderline for small body text. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller sizes drop below the threshold.
mediumseagreen: Medium Sea Green is a deep, slightly blue-leaning mid-green that reads as natural and grounded. The CSS hex sits between Sea Green and Forest Green, with enough chroma to feel intentional without veering into saturation. Outdoor, wellness, and environmental brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream and terracotta for editorial palettes, or with charcoal for a quieter professional look. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably. The chroma level is balanced enough that adjacent saturated accents will not produce visual vibration against it, which makes it a reliable primary brand color.
mediumslateblue: Medium Slate Blue is a saturated mid-purple-blue with strong presence at moderate lightness. The CSS hex reads as confident and slightly modern, useful as a primary brand color for tech, education, or premium-service products. Pair with cream and deep gold for a slightly luxurious palette, or with charcoal for type contrast. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines and most body text. Reserve large-area use for hero sections or accent panels rather than full-page backgrounds; the chroma level can compete with foreground content in dense layouts. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes.
mediumspringgreen: Medium Spring Green is the saturated mint of new growth in early spring. The CSS hex is bright and slightly cool, almost neon. Use it as an accent only; large fields of Medium Spring Green fatigue the eye quickly and rarely pass body text contrast against white. Pair with deep navy or charcoal for type, or with magenta for a complementary high-energy clash. As a UI element it works for success states and emphasized buttons with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; the value works only as a surface or accent color.
mediumturquoise: Medium Turquoise is the bright, slightly cyan-leaning turquoise of swimming-pool tiles. The CSS hex is slightly muted compared to pure Turquoise and works well as a primary brand color for travel, beauty, and wellness products. Pair with cream and warm coral for a fresh tropical palette, or with deep navy for tonal contrast. Against white it passes contrast for headlines and large buttons; check body text use at smaller sizes. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve text use for headlines and rely on dark text overlays for surfaces.
mediumvioletred: Medium Violet Red is a deep, slightly pink-leaning magenta with strong chroma at mid lightness. The CSS hex reads as confident and slightly theatrical, useful as a primary accent for cosmetic, fashion, or arts brands. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with deep teal for a complementary contrast. Against white it passes contrast for headlines and most body text. Reserve large-area use for hero sections; the saturation makes it heavy for full-page backgrounds. Body text contrast against white passes AA at body sizes; AAA holds at headline sizes only, so verify smaller text carefully.
midnightblue: Midnight Blue is the deep, slightly purple-leaning navy of a clear night sky away from city lights. The CSS hex is one of the darker named colors in the spec and sits between Navy and Dark Slate Blue. Heritage brands in finance, aviation, and luxury use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm brass for a luxury accent. Against white it passes AAA body text contrast comfortably and works well for long-form reading. The slight purple lean distinguishes it from pure Navy in side-by-side viewing but the difference is subtle enough that the two are often interchangeable.
mintcream: Mint Cream is a pale, slightly green-tinted off-white that reads as fresh and clean. The CSS hex sits in the territory of medical, dental, and wellness brand backgrounds. Pair with deep teal for type, or with charcoal for a quieter palette. Against pure white the difference is subtle, so it works best as a layered surface or panel background. The cool tint distinguishes it from warm off-whites like Floral White or Linen, which carry yellow undertones instead. Two Mint Cream surfaces in adjacent layers will produce a near-invisible boundary in dim viewing conditions, so layer with care.
mistyrose: Misty Rose is one of the gentler pinks in the CSS spec, a very pale warm pink with a slight blue cast. The hex reads as restrained and slightly nostalgic. Beauty, wellness, and stationery brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep burgundy for type, or with charcoal for a more neutral palette. Against pure white it provides modest separation; layer it carefully against other pale pinks to avoid muddying. Two Misty Rose surfaces side by side will look identical in dim viewing; treat the color as a single-layer tone rather than for nested card layouts.
moccasin: Moccasin is the warm tan of soft leather, named after the indigenous North American footwear. The CSS hex sits between Peach Puff and Wheat, with low chroma and a yellow-orange undertone. Outdoor, craft, and heritage brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep brown for type, or with muted forest green for a softer secondary accent. The warm cast makes it especially comfortable for product photography backgrounds in leather goods, handcrafts, and apparel. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; reserve it as a surface tone with dark text overlays.
mustard: Mustard is the curated deep yellow named after the condiment. The hex is more saturated than CSS Khaki and warmer than Goldenrod, with strong harvest associations. Editorial, food, and heritage brands use it as a primary accent or display heading color. Pair with deep navy for type, or with cream and brass for warm autumn palettes. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines but is borderline for body text. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller text in Mustard against a white background will appear washed out and lose legibility quickly.
navajowhite: Navajo White is a warm, slightly pink-yellow off-white named after a Diné textile palette. The CSS hex reads as quiet and inviting, distinct from cooler whites. Editorial, interior, and craft brands use it as a primary background. Pair with deep brown for type, or with terracotta for a softer secondary accent. The warm cast suits long-form reading and product photography where a hint of fabric texture is wanted without committing to a real color. Body text on a Navajo White surface should use a deep neutral type color; cool grays will read as slightly off against the warm cast.
navy: Navy is one of the oldest color words in English, named after the dark blue uniforms of military fleets. The CSS hex is identical to Dark Blue almost by accident, both inheriting from the same X11 source. It carries authoritative, professional, and heritage associations across cultures. Use it as a primary identity color for financial, legal, or editorial products. Pair with cream and warm gold for traditional palettes, or with bright coral for unexpected energy. Body text contrast against white is excellent. Navy passes AAA at every standard size and remains one of the most legible saturated colors in the CSS spec.
oldlace: Old Lace was added to the X11 colour list in 1989 specifically as the warmest off-white in the palette - a step beyond Antique White toward the colour of cotton lace that has yellowed in a glass-fronted cabinet. The CSS value still does that job well. Editorial designers at Kinfolk and Cereal magazine reach for tones in this band when they want a background that reads as analogue without going full cream. Pair it with chocolate `#3d2817` for body copy and the layout takes on the warmth of a letterpress invitation. Cool greys placed adjacent will read as slightly out of register against the warm cast.
olive: Olive in CSS is the deep yellow-green of cured olives and Mediterranean foliage. The hex is darker and less saturated than the dictionary version of olive and sits firmly in the yellow family by hue. Editorial, heritage, and outdoor brands use it as a primary accent. Pair with cream for warmth, or with terracotta for complementary depth. Against white it carries enough contrast for body text in most sizes. Body text in CSS Olive against white passes AA at body sizes; AAA holds only at sizes 18px and above. The undertone gives it more presence than CSS Olive Drab at the same size.
olivedrab: Olive Drab is the official colour the US Army standardised for combat uniforms from 1902 through Vietnam, replacing the bright blue that proved disastrous in low-vegetation conflicts. The CSS value matches the wartime spec closely: muted, slightly greyed, the colour of canvas tents under midday sun. Filson and Carhartt have used surfaces in this band for catalogue work since long before either brand had a marketing department. Pair it with warm cream `#fef3c7` for a softer palette or with brick red for the surplus-store aesthetic that Engineered Garments built a decade of collections around. Body text against white passes AA at sizes 16px and above.
orange: Orange is the warm, mid-saturation orange of citrus fruit at peak ripeness. The CSS hex carries high lightness and chroma, making it one of the more energetic mid-tones in the spec. Cultural associations are appetite, energy, autumn, and warmth. Use it as a primary brand color for food, sports, or outdoor products. Pair with deep navy or charcoal for type contrast; avoid placing it next to bright red, which produces visual conflict. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve text use for headlines and use dark text overlays whenever it appears as a surface or button.
orangered: Orange Red is the colour of a propane flame at its hottest visible band, and the CSS value carries that urgency. Reddit picked a hex in this family for its upvote button in 2008 because the eye locks onto it without conscious effort - useful for an action that needs a single decisive click. The flip side is that large fields of Orange Red fatigue the retina within seconds. Reserve it for primary CTAs against a deep navy `#1e3a8a` page or as type on cream. Body text in Orange Red against white falls below AA at body sizes; the value should function as accent rather than as copy.
orchid: Orchid is the bright, slightly pink-leaning mid-purple named after the flower. The CSS hex is brighter than Medium Orchid and reads as theatrical and slightly nostalgic. Cosmetic, fashion, and arts brands use it as a primary accent. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with deep teal for a richer contrast. As a UI element it works well for buttons and display headings; against white it carries enough contrast for most non-body uses but is borderline for small text. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller text drops below.
palegoldenrod: Pale Goldenrod is the pale, faintly green-yellow cream of dried summer flowers. The CSS hex sits between Lemon Chiffon and Khaki, with low chroma and a warm undertone. Editorial, food, and wellness brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep brown for type, or with muted forest green for a softer secondary accent. Against pure white the difference is subtle, so it works best as a layered surface rather than as a primary page background. Two Pale Goldenrod surfaces in adjacent layers will produce a barely-visible boundary that can read as banding rather than as intentional separation.
palegreen: Pale Green is a soft, slightly yellow-leaning green at high lightness and moderate chroma. The CSS hex reads as fresh and friendly, useful as a background for wellness, food, and outdoor brands. Pair with deep green or warm brown for type, or with cream for a softer palette. Against white it provides reasonable separation as a panel background but is too pale for body text. As a UI element it works well for success states and emphasized panels with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; reserve it strictly for surfaces.
paleturquoise: Pale Turquoise is the very pale, slightly cyan-leaning turquoise of shallow tropical water seen from above. The CSS hex carries low chroma at high lightness and reads as calm and slightly nostalgic. Travel, wellness, and beauty brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep teal for type, or with charcoal for a more neutral palette. Against pure white the difference is subtle, so it works best as a layered surface or panel background rather than as a primary page color. Two Pale Turquoise surfaces in adjacent layers will appear identical in dim viewing.
palevioletred: Pale Violet Red is the muted, slightly pink-leaning red-purple of dusty flowers. The CSS hex sits between Light Pink and Medium Violet Red, with moderate chroma at mid-high lightness. Editorial, beauty, and floral brands use it as a primary or secondary accent. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with deep burgundy for tonal depth. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines and large buttons but is borderline for body text. Body text contrast against white passes AA at sizes 18px and above; below that, step down to a deeper rose for type use.
papayawhip: Papaya Whip is the pale, slightly orange-yellow cream named after the tropical fruit dessert. The CSS hex sits between Bisque and Lemon Chiffon, with low chroma and a warm undertone. Food, beauty, and craft brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep brown for type, or with terracotta for a warmer secondary accent. The warm cast suits product photography and food packaging where a hint of warmth is wanted without committing to a real color. Adjacent cool grays will read as slightly off-register against the warm cast, so layer with awareness.
peachpuff: Peach Puff is the pale, warm pink-orange of a ripe peach skin. The CSS hex sits between Bisque and Light Salmon, with low chroma and a sunny undertone. Beauty, wellness, and editorial brands use it as a quiet background or secondary surface. Pair with deep teal for type, or with warm brown for a softer secondary accent. As a UI element it works as a panel background for cosmetic product cards; against white it provides reasonable separation but is too pale for body text. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every size; reserve it as a surface tone.
peru: Peru is the warm tan-brown named loosely after the South American country, though the connection is decorative rather than geographic. The CSS hex sits in the brown family at mid-lightness with moderate warmth. Heritage, outdoor, and craft brands use it as a primary accent. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with muted forest green for a heritage look. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines and most body text in larger sizes. Body text contrast against white passes AA at sizes 14px and above; below that, the warm undertone starts to lose legibility against the cool white background.
pink: Pink in CSS is the soft, slightly warm pink of cotton candy. The hex is one of the gentler pinks in the spec, lighter than Hot Pink and warmer than Light Pink. Beauty, wellness, and fashion brands use it as a primary or secondary surface. Pair with deep burgundy for type, or with muted teal for a calmer secondary accent. Cultural associations are wide-ranging and have shifted significantly over the past century; pink has been masculine and feminine in different eras and regions. Use it with awareness of context.
