Body text contrast against white fails AA; reserve as a surface tone.
plum: The English word plum carries two associated colours: the deep purple-black of the fruit's skin and the dusty pinkish-grey of the dried flesh inside. CSS Plum captures the latter. The X11 committee picked the value from the Crayola box in 1985 and froze it; modern Crayola Plum has since drifted darker. The CSS hex works well as a heritage accent - the kind Aesop uses on apothecary labels - or as a secondary surface in editorial. Pair it with cream and brushed gold for an art-deco palette. Body text against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; for smaller copy step down to a deeper purple.
powderblue: Powder Blue is the pale, slightly cyan-leaning blue of soft baby blanket fabric. The CSS hex sits between Light Blue and Pale Turquoise, with low chroma at high lightness. Beauty, wellness, and baby-product brands use it as a quiet background. Pair with deep navy for type, or with charcoal for a more neutral palette. Against pure white the difference is subtle, so it works best as a layered surface or panel background. The cool undertone suits medical and clinical branding contexts as well, where a hint of fresh air is wanted without committing to a saturated accent.
purple: Purple in CSS is #800080, the deep historical purple of Roman ceremonial robes dyed with murex shellfish. The hex sits at mid-low lightness with strong chroma in the magenta-purple zone. Cultural associations are weighty: royalty, mourning, ceremony, religion, music. Use it as a primary identity color for arts, premium-service, or heritage brands. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm gold for a luxury accent. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably. Body text in CSS Purple passes AAA at standard sizes; the chroma level can still feel intense in long-form text and is best for short copy.
rebeccapurple: Rebecca Purple was added to the CSS spec in 2014 in memory of Rebecca Meyer, the six-year-old daughter of CSS author Eric Meyer, who died of a brain tumour the day after her sixth birthday. The hex is a dignified mid-purple, darker than CSS Purple, and carries the weight of its history. Body text against white passes AAA at every standard size, which makes it usable as a primary type colour in long-form layouts where the gravitas suits the work.
red: Red in CSS is #ff0000, the brightest possible red the sRGB color space can produce. The hex is too saturated for almost any practical UI use except as a brief error state or attention flag. Cultural associations are unusually strong: danger, passion, urgency, importance. Real-world red branding usually pulls a slightly desaturated or darker variant. Use the pure value strictly as a visual stop signal; for sustained use, prefer Crimson, Firebrick, or a modern OKLCH-tuned red. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; reserve the pure value for surfaces and brief accent moments.
rosybrown: Rosy Brown is the colour weathered terracotta turns after a few seasons of rain - warm, slightly desaturated, with the pink undertone that distinguishes it from the deeper Saddle Brown of the spec. The X11 list pulled the name from a Crayola palette and the value has been stable since. Brands working in skincare and natural cosmetics, such as Aesop and Tata Harper, lean on tones in this band because the warmth reads as honest rather than glossy. Pair it with deep forest green for a vintage botanical feel. Body text against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; reserve smaller copy for a deeper warm brown.
royalblue: Royal Blue is the bright, slightly purple-leaning blue historically associated with British monarchy. The CSS hex carries strong chroma at mid lightness and reads as confident and slightly formal. Editorial, financial, and heritage brands use it as a primary accent. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm gold for a luxury accent. Against white it passes body text contrast in most sizes and works well as a primary action color or display heading. Body text in Royal Blue against white passes AA at standard body sizes and AAA at sizes 18px and above.
saddlebrown: Saddle Brown is the deep, warm brown of a well-worn leather saddle. The CSS hex sits at low lightness with moderate chroma and reads as grounded, heritage, and slightly nostalgic. Outdoor, leather, and craft brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with muted forest green for a heritage outdoor palette. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably and works well as a primary type color or display heading. Body text in Saddle Brown against white passes AAA at every standard size; the value carries long-form reading confidently.
salmon: Salmon is the warm pink-orange of fresh-caught Atlantic salmon flesh. The CSS hex carries moderate chroma at mid-high lightness and reads as friendly without being aggressive. Beauty, wellness, and editorial brands use it as a primary or secondary accent. Pair with deep teal for type contrast, or with cream for a softer palette. Against white it carries enough contrast for headlines but is borderline for body text. As a UI element it works well for warmth-focused buttons and panels with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes.
sandybrown: Sandy Brown is the warm orange-tan of dry beach sand at midday, brighter than Burlywood and lighter than Peru. seafoam: Seafoam is the curated pale mint-green of ocean foam. The hex sits between Mint Cream and Pale Green, with low-moderate chroma and a slight blue cast. seagreen: Sea Green is the deep, slightly blue-leaning green of a calm ocean at midday. The CSS hex sits between Forest Green and Light Sea Green, with moderate chroma at low-mid lightness. Against white it passes body text contrast in most sizes and works well as a primary action color or display heading. seashell: Seashell is one of the warmest off-whites in the CSS spec, with a faint pink-orange undertone. The hex reads as quiet and inviting, distinct from cooler whites. The warm cast suits long-form reading and product photography where a hint of texture is wanted without committing to a real color. Two Seashell surfaces sitting adjacent will look identical in dim viewing conditions, so treat the color as a single-layer tone rather than for nested cards.
sienna: Sienna is the warm red-brown of iron-rich earth fired into pigment, named after the Tuscan town. The CSS hex sits at low-mid lightness with strong warmth and moderate chroma. Heritage, art, and craft brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with muted forest green for an editorial palette. Against white it passes body text contrast comfortably and works well as a primary type color or display heading. silver: Silver in CSS is a mid-light gray, originally chosen to approximate the metallic finish in non-glossy contexts. The hex sits at lightness 0.75 with no chroma. Technology, luxury, and automotive brands use it as a metallic stand-in. Pair with deep charcoal for type and a single saturated accent. As a UI element it works for borders and quiet surfaces. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at every standard size; the value cannot function as text against white and should be reserved for borders, surfaces, and decorative elements with dark text overlays.
skyblue: Sky Blue is the colour of a clear midday sky away from the horizon haze, the same blue Maxfield Parrish captured in his 1920s magazine work - known thereafter as Parrish Blue. The CSS hex is brighter and slightly cooler than the painted version but holds the same emotional weight. Wellness and travel brands use surfaces in this band as page washes - Calm and Headspace both use neighbouring values. Pair it with navy `#1e3a8a` body copy or with cream for a warmer layout. Body text in Sky Blue against white falls below AA at every size; treat it as a surface tone, not a type colour.
slateblue: Slate Blue is the muted, slightly purple-leaning blue-gray of stratified rock. The CSS hex sits at mid-low lightness with moderate chroma in the indigo zone. Editorial, financial, and architectural brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm gold for a heritage accent. Against white it passes body text contrast in most sizes. As a UI element it works well as a primary action color or as a sophisticated alternative to standard navy. Body text in Slate Blue against white passes AA at body sizes; AAA holds at headline sizes.
slategray: Slate Gray is the cool, slightly blue-leaning mid-gray of stratified rock. The CSS hex carries low chroma at mid lightness and reads as restrained and considered. Editorial, architectural, and business brands use it as a primary or secondary neutral. Pair with cream for a softer palette, or with warm coral for an unexpected secondary accent. As a UI element it works well for secondary text, borders, and de-emphasized labels. Against white it carries enough contrast for body text at standard sizes. Body text in Slate Gray against white passes AA at body sizes but not AAA at small sizes.
snow: Snow is one of the palest off-whites in CSS, with a faint pink-warm undertone that distinguishes it from pure white. Pair with deep charcoal for type, or with warm brown for a softer secondary accent. Two Snow surfaces side by side will look identical in dim viewing conditions; treat the color as a single-layer tone rather than for nested cards or stacked surfaces.
springgreen: Spring Green is the brilliant, slightly cyan-leaning green of new shoots in early spring. The CSS hex is one of the most saturated greens in the spec and reads as almost neon. Use it strictly as an accent; large fields of Spring Green fatigue the eye quickly and rarely pass body text contrast against white. Pair with deep navy for type, or with magenta for a complementary clash. As a UI element it works for success states and emphasized buttons with dark text overlays. steelblue: Steel Blue is the muted, slightly cyan-leaning mid-blue of, originally, polished steel under cool light. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm coral for an unexpected accent. Against white it passes body text contrast in most sizes and works well as a sophisticated alternative to brighter blues. tailwind-amber-500: Tailwind Amber 500 anchors the framework's amber palette at mid-saturation, leaning slightly toward orange. tailwind-amber-600: Tailwind Amber 600 lands in the colour bourbon takes on under direct light - warm, slightly brown, just past mustard. tailwind-blue-500: Tailwind Blue 500 is one of the most-deployed blues on the modern web, sitting at the center of Tailwind's blue ramp. tailwind-blue-600: Step into Tailwind Blue 600 and the palette gets a colour with the same emotional register as the Stripe brand blue - deeper than the famously friendly Tailwind 500, more committed without going corporate. It pairs cleanly with Tailwind Slate 50 surfaces for type and with a single warm accent like Amber 500 for emphasis. Body text against white passes AA comfortably at every standard size and reaches AAA at 16px and above, which makes it a safe default for primary copy.
tailwind-cyan-500: Tailwind Cyan 500 sits at the saturated mid-cyan center of Tailwind v4's cyan ramp. Use it for informational accents, data visualization, or as a secondary brand color in technology and communications products. Against white the contrast is borderline for body text at small sizes; reserve text use for headlines and rely on dark text overlays when the color appears as a button or panel surface. tailwind-cyan-600: Tailwind Cyan 600 is the cyan you reach for when the brand needs to feel technical without going icy. tailwind-fuchsia-500: Tailwind Fuchsia 500 sits at the saturated bright pink-purple center of Tailwind v4's fuchsia ramp. tailwind-fuchsia-600: Fuchsia 600 lives in the same band Glossier picked for the original You bottle in 2017 - saturated, slightly cool, the kind of digital pink that reads as adult rather than as Barbie. tailwind-gray-500: Tailwind Gray 500 anchors the framework's gray ramp at mid-light, carrying a faint blue undertone. tailwind-gray-600: Gray 600 is Tailwind's workhorse body-type colour for any product that wants to feel calm and current - the value Linear, Vercel's marketing site, and Cal.com all sit within a few delta-E units of for their default body copy. tailwind-green-500: Tailwind Green 500 sits at the saturated mid-green center of Tailwind v4's green ramp. The hex falls between Emerald and Lime in hue and reads as fresh and energetic. tailwind-indigo-600: Indigo 600 is the hex Tailwind picked as the default brand accent in its v1 marketing site, and the choice rippled through hundreds of imitators in the years that followed. tailwind-lime-500: Tailwind Lime 500 is the bright yellow-green that anchors the framework's lime ramp. tailwind-pink-600: Pink 600 is the hex Glossier might have shipped if it had launched in 2023 rather than 2014 - slightly deeper than the period-defining Glossier Millennial Pink, modernised by a small push toward magenta. The Tailwind value is dark enough to function as body type against white, which sets it apart from the pastels above it in the ramp. Beauty and youth-targeted SaaS brands use tones in this band as primary identity colour. Pair it with a deep teal accent or with a warm cream for surfaces. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes; AAA holds at 16px and above for headline emphasis use.
tailwind-purple-500: Tailwind Purple 500 anchors the framework's purple ramp at mid-saturation. The hex sits between Violet and Fuchsia in hue and reads as confident and slightly theatrical. Use it as a primary brand color for premium services, arts, or technology products. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for larger sizes and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller body text needs the 600 stop for legible contrast.
tailwind-purple-600: Purple 600 is the colour of premium and the colour of trouble - Cadbury, Asprey, and FedEx have all leaned on tones in this band for primary identity work. Tailwind's hex pulls slightly toward magenta compared with the framework's Violet 600, which gives it a warmer presence than the cooler Indigo 600 next door. Use it as a primary brand colour for arts-and-culture products or as a hover state above Purple 500 buttons. Pair it with a chartreuse accent for a Cadbury-style poster palette or with charcoal for type-heavy layouts. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes and AAA at 16px and above.
tailwind-red-500: Tailwind Red 500 sits at the saturated mid-red center of the framework's red ramp. The v4 hex was retuned in OKLCH space from the v3 value and reads as slightly cooler and more confident than its predecessor. Use it for destructive actions, error states, and high-urgency call-to-action buttons in dashboards and forms. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for larger sizes and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above.
tailwind-red-600: Red 600 is the destructive-action red of choice in modern UI - slightly deeper than Tailwind 500, calibrated to pass body-text contrast against white at standard sizes, and dark enough that a button styled with it reads as serious rather than playful. The Vercel dashboard, Linear, and most post-2020 SaaS products use tones within a few delta-E of this hex for delete confirmations and error states. Pair it with a calm Slate 50 surface to keep the red from feeling alarming in non-urgent contexts. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes and AAA at 18px and above, which makes it usable for error message copy.
tailwind-rose-500: Tailwind Rose 500 anchors the framework's rose ramp at the saturated red-pink center. The hex sits slightly warmer than Pink 500 and slightly cooler than Red 500, with high chroma at mid-high lightness. Use it as a primary brand color for cosmetic, fashion, or hospitality products. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for headlines and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller body text needs the 600 stop or deeper for legibility.
tailwind-rose-600: Rose 600 is the cooler, more grown-up sibling of the framework's Pink 600 - pulled slightly toward red and away from magenta, the colour Aesop and Glossier both have palette entries adjacent to. The Tailwind hex is dark enough to function as body type against white, which the lighter rose stops cannot do. Cosmetics, hospitality, and editorial brands use tones in this band when they need a pink that reads as adult rather than playful. Pair it with a deep teal accent for a complementary palette or with warm cream for a softer surface. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes.
tailwind-sky-500: Tailwind Sky 500 sits at the bright, slightly cyan-leaning blue center of the framework's sky ramp. The hex falls between Cyan and Blue in hue, brighter than Blue 500 and slightly warmer than Cyan 500. Use it as a primary brand color for travel, weather, or aviation products. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for headlines and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller body text needs the 600 stop.
tailwind-sky-600: Sky 600 is the colour of a clear sky an hour before sunset, with just enough cyan to keep it from feeling navy. Tailwind's hex is the headline blue for travel and weather products - the Citymapper app, Klima, and Backblaze all sit within a few delta-E of this neighbourhood. The depth puts it on the legible side of the contrast line against white at standard sizes. Pair it with warm coral `#fb7185` for the kind of palette ridesharing apps have used since 2020 or with a single charcoal accent for type. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes and AAA at 16px and above.
tailwind-slate-500: Tailwind Slate 500 anchors the framework's slate ramp at mid-light. The hex carries a slight blue undertone that distinguishes it from warmer neutrals like Stone or Zinc at the same stop. Use it for secondary type, borders, and disabled-state surfaces in technology and business products. The cool cast pairs especially well with blue accent colors and keeps a palette feeling modern. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard body sizes; AAA holds only at sizes 18px and above for de-emphasized labels and footnote text use.
tailwind-slate-600: Slate 600 is the cool grey Vercel, Supabase, and most other dev-tool sites of the last five years use for body type - just enough blue cast to feel modern but never crossing into looking tinted. The hex sits in the same neighbourhood Apple's macOS uses for system labels in light mode. Body text against white passes AAA at every standard size, which is what makes it such a common default for primary copy in design systems built around blue or indigo brand colours. Pair it with any chromatic accent in the cool half of the wheel; warm accents work, though the temperature contrast becomes dominant.
tailwind-stone-500: Tailwind Stone 500 anchors the framework's stone ramp at mid-light. The hex carries a slight yellow undertone that distinguishes it from cooler neutrals like Slate or Zinc at the same stop. Use it for secondary type, borders, and disabled-state surfaces in editorial, hospitality, or heritage products. The warm cast pairs especially well with terracotta and brass for accents and with cream surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard body sizes; AAA holds only at sizes 18px and above for de-emphasized text and small label use.
tailwind-stone-600: Stone 600 is the warm grey of weathered limestone, with just enough brown undertone to pair cleanly with terracotta and brass without colour clash. Editorial and hospitality brands - magazines like Cereal and hotel chains like Ace - use tones in this band for body copy because the warmth softens long-form reading in a way cool greys cannot. Pair it with a Tailwind Stone 50 surface and any warm accent in the orange-to-rose band. Body text against white passes AAA at every standard size, making Stone 600 a confident copy colour for layouts in warm-palette designs where Slate or Gray would read as too clinical.
tailwind-teal-500: Tailwind Teal 500 sits at the saturated mid-teal center of the framework's teal ramp. The hex falls between Emerald and Cyan in hue and reads as confident and slightly fresh. Use it as a primary brand color for technology, wellness, or environmental products. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for larger sizes and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller body text needs the 600 stop for legibility.
tailwind-teal-600: Teal 600 is the colour Headspace shipped in its 2022 rebrand and that wellness brands have orbited around ever since. Tailwind's hex pulls slightly cooler than that brand value but lives in the same emotional band - calm, slightly mineral, neither obviously blue nor obviously green. The depth lets it function as body type against white at standard sizes. Use it as a primary identity colour for environmental, wellness, or biotech products. Pair it with a warm coral `#fb7185` accent for a complementary palette that avoids the obvious teal-and-orange tech-startup template. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes and AAA at 18px and above.
tailwind-violet-500: Tailwind Violet 500 anchors the framework's violet ramp at mid-saturation. The hex sits between Indigo and Purple in hue and reads as confident and slightly modern. Use it as a primary brand color for technology, premium services, or arts products. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for larger sizes and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller body text needs the 600 stop or darker for legibility against white surfaces.
tailwind-violet-600: Violet 600 is the colour Linear uses for its primary brand accent, and Notion sits within a few delta-E units of the same hex on its workspace icon. Tailwind's value pulls slightly bluer than Purple 600 and reads as more technical, less regal. Use it as a primary identity colour for productivity tools or as a hover state above Violet 500 buttons. Pair it with a warm cream surface for editorial layouts or with chartreuse for a poster-shop palette. Body text against white passes AA at body sizes and AAA at 16px and above, which makes Violet 600 safe for primary copy.
tailwind-yellow-500: Tailwind Yellow 500 sits at the saturated mid-yellow center of the framework's yellow ramp. The hex carries very high chroma and reads as a confident harvest yellow. Use it as a primary brand color for food, energy, or attention-focused products, or as a warning state in dashboards and forms. Against white the body text contrast fails AA at all sizes; reserve it strictly for backgrounds, fills, and accents with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every standard size; the value works only as a surface or marker, never as type.
tailwind-yellow-600: Yellow 600 is the hex Tailwind sits at when the framework's yellow finally crosses from highlighter into bronze-amber territory. The value is dark enough to pass body-text contrast at headline sizes but still borderline at 14-16px. Caterpillar machinery and the older National Geographic logos both use yellows in this neighbourhood; the depth reads as utilitarian rather than as cheerful. Use it as a brand accent for industrial, energy, or heritage products. Pair it with deep teal `#0f766e` for a complementary palette or with charcoal `#1f2937` for type-heavy layouts. Body text against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller copy loses legibility against any pale surface.
tailwind-zinc-500: Tailwind Zinc 500 anchors the framework's zinc ramp at mid-light. The hex carries a faint blue-purple undertone that distinguishes it from the bluer Slate ramp and the warmer Stone ramp at the same stop. Use it for secondary type, borders, and disabled-state surfaces in modern technology products. The slight purple cast pairs especially well with violet and fuchsia accent colors. Body text contrast against white passes AA at standard body sizes; AAA holds only at sizes 18px and above for de-emphasized labels and small footnote text use.
tailwind-zinc-600: Zinc 600 is the cool grey of a galvanised steel countertop after a few years of use - slightly more saturated and faintly purple compared with Tailwind's true-neutral Gray 600. Linear and Raycast both use tones in this band for body type because the faint cool cast pairs cleanly with violet and indigo brand accents without temperature clash. Body text against white passes AAA at every standard size, making it a confident copy colour for products that want to feel modern and slightly engineered rather than warm. Pair it with any chromatic accent in the cool half of the wheel for the smoothest temperature story.
tan: Tan in CSS is the warm, slightly orange-leaning beige of dried grass. The hex sits between Wheat and Burlywood, with low chroma at high lightness. Outdoor, heritage, and craft brands use it as a quiet primary or secondary surface. Pair with deep brown for type, or with muted forest green for a heritage outdoor palette. As a UI element it works as a panel background for outdoor product cards; against white it provides reasonable separation but is too pale for body text. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every standard size; reserve it as a surface tone.
teal: Teal in CSS is the deep, slightly blue-leaning green-blue of the duck for which it was named. The hex sits at low-mid lightness with moderate chroma and reads as confident and slightly nostalgic. Editorial, wellness, and technology brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm coral for a complementary accent. Against white it passes body text contrast in most sizes and works well as a primary action color or display heading. Body text in CSS Teal against white passes AAA at every standard size for confident long-form layout use.
terracotta: Terracotta - Italian for cooked earth - has named the same colour since Roman roof tiles were first fired around 100 BC. The curated hex in this list lands on the warmer, more orange end of the band rather than on the brown-leaning Tuscan tile colour. Heritage food brands, garden suppliers, and the long lineage of slow-fashion labels including Toast and Mara Hoffman all use surfaces in this neighbourhood as primary identity colour. Pair it with sage green or cream for a Mediterranean palette that avoids the obvious red-and-cream cliché. Body text against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; reserve smaller copy for a deeper warm brown.
thistle: Thistle is the pale, dusty mid-purple-pink of the spiny wildflower's bloom. The CSS hex sits between Lavender and Plum, with low chroma at high lightness. Beauty, wellness, and floral brands use it as a quiet primary or secondary surface. Pair with deep purple for type, or with charcoal for a more neutral palette. As a UI element it works as a panel background; against pure white it provides modest separation, so use it with awareness of the layering. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every standard size; reserve it strictly as a surface tone.
tomato: Tomato is the bright, slightly orange-leaning red of a ripe summer tomato. The CSS hex carries high chroma at mid-high lightness and reads as energetic and warm. Food, sports, and outdoor brands use it as a primary or accent color. Pair with deep navy for type contrast, or with cream for a softer palette. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for larger sizes and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above.
turquoise: Turquoise is the bright, slightly cyan-leaning teal of the gemstone seen at peak quality. The CSS hex sits between Light Sea Green and Dark Turquoise, with strong chroma at mid-high lightness. Travel, beauty, and wellness brands use it as a primary identity color. Pair with cream for warmth, or with warm coral for a complementary accent. Against white the body text contrast is borderline; reserve text use for larger sizes and rely on dark text overlays for buttons. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at body sizes; the value works only at headline sizes.
violet: Violet in CSS is the bright, slightly pink-leaning purple of the wild flower. The hex sits between Orchid and Plum, with moderate chroma at mid-high lightness. Editorial, beauty, and arts brands use it as a primary or secondary accent. Pair with cream for warmth, or with deep teal for a complementary contrast. Against white the body text contrast is borderline at small sizes; reserve text use for headlines and use dark text overlays on button surfaces. Body text contrast against white passes AA only at sizes 18px and above; smaller text needs a deeper purple.
wheat: Wheat is the colour of mature grain heads in late summer, the same hue Pieter Bruegel painted across the foreground of his 1565 Harvesters canvas. The CSS value, drawn from the X11 list in the 1980s, sits between Cornsilk and Tan with low chroma and high lightness. Food brands like King Arthur Flour and Bob's Red Mill use surfaces in this band on packaging because the warmth photographs as natural rather than as manufactured. Pair it with walnut `#3e2817` for type or with muted forest green for a heritage outdoor palette. Body text against white fails AA at every standard size; treat Wheat strictly as a surface tone.
white: White in CSS is #ffffff, the full on-state of every pixel in the sRGB color space. As a UI surface it carries no inherent emotional load, which is exactly why it is the default background for the majority of websites and applications. Used too aggressively, especially in large fields under tungsten light, it can produce eye fatigue; many modern designs reach for a slightly warmer off-white like Snow, Ivory, or a Stone 50 instead. Pair with any dark type and any accent color. Two pure white surfaces in adjacent layers will be indistinguishable without separator borders.
whitesmoke: White Smoke is the layered-surface off-white most modern dashboards use for card backgrounds - Stripe, Linear, and the Vercel dashboard all sit within a few delta-E of this hex for their secondary surfaces. The CSS value carries the faintest warm cast, just enough to read as a distinct layer above pure white without competing for attention. Pair it with charcoal `#1c1917` body copy and any chromatic accent. Two White Smoke surfaces stacked directly on top of each other will read as the same layer under low-light viewing conditions, so introduce a 1px border or a Slate 100 step when nesting cards more than one level deep.
yellow: Yellow in CSS is #ffff00, the brightest possible yellow the sRGB color space can produce. The hex is too saturated for almost any practical UI use except as a brief attention marker. Cultural associations are strong: sun, caution, cheer, cowardice, depending on region. Real-world yellow branding usually pulls a slightly desaturated or darker variant like Goldenrod or Mustard for actual deployment. As body text against white the contrast fails completely; reserve it strictly for backgrounds, fills, and decorative accents with dark text overlays. Body text contrast against white fails AA at every standard size.
yellowgreen: Yellow Green is the bright chartreuse-leaning green that sits halfway between yellow and green at full saturation. The CSS hex carries high chroma at mid-high lightness and reads as fresh and energetic. Use it as an accent only; large fields of Yellow Green fatigue the eye quickly. Pair with deep navy for type, or with magenta for a complementary clash. As a UI element it works well for emphasized buttons and panels with dark text overlays in sports and food branding. Body text contrast against white falls below AA at every standard size.
